skippedSources.name: Capital Flows Research (capitalflows@substack.com)
skippedSources.reason: Primarily a livestream promo — no extractable analytical content in email body beyond credit-cycle-melt-up framing already covered by TMAD and Dark Side of the Boom on Warsh

items.title: US-Iran MOU Signed: Ceasefire Sealed, Hormuz Reopening, $25B Frozen Assets Released
items.idea: The US and Iran signed a Memorandum of Understanding on June 16, solidifying the ceasefire and framing 60 days of nuclear talks brokered by Pakistan and Qatar. The deal includes reopening the Strait of Hormuz and releasing $25B of Iran's frozen assets — the naval blockade is lifting now. Formal ceremony set for June 19 in Switzerland. War lasted ~3.5 months (began Feb 28). Deal terms 'remain murky'; Trump warned it could be called off if Iran won't shut its nuclear program. Iran has not finalized every term — verification and uranium handling still being negotiated.
items.angle: Oil already pricing this in aggressively (WTI below $80). The real trade is now in the 2nd-order: airlines, refiners on crack spread normalization, energy producers net short. But the 'embers remain' warning is real — every prior Middle East ceasefire had a trigger point. Do not confuse ceasefire with resolution.
items.tweetA: US-Iran MOU is signed. Hormuz opens. $25B in frozen assets released. The war is over — for now. Oil is below $80 and the market is pricing perfection. Three things still not resolved: uranium verification, Trump's 'could be called off' warning, and Israel hitting Hezbollah targets through the weekend. Relief trades work until they don't.
items.tweetB: The US-Iran war lasted 3.5 months and ended with Pakistan brokering the ceasefire. Pakistan. The geopolitical architecture of the Middle East just rewired. Saudi, Qatar, Turkey credited. Iran not finalized on every term. Formal ceremony June 19 in Switzerland. This is a pause, not an end. Price accordingly.

items.title: Housing Starts Collapse -15.4% in May — Lowest Since May 2020
items.idea: US housing starts fell 15.4% in May to 1.177M annualized — the weakest reading since May 2020. This is a significant data point arriving the day before Warsh's first FOMC decision. It hands Warsh a growth-is-cracking argument to counterbalance the sticky 4.2% CPI and 6.5% PPI YoY. Combined with oil below $80 draining the inflation impulse, this shifts the hawkish-hold thesis to something softer. Market is pricing 97.4% hold — all the action is in the dot plot and tone.
items.angle: Housing is the leading edge of the credit transmission mechanism. A 15% one-month collapse at 6% mortgage rates is exactly the lagged effect the Fed expected — but arriving now, into a FOMC week, it gives Warsh political cover to avoid the hawkish framing markets feared. Watch how he interprets this data vs the CPI.
items.tweetA: Housing starts just collapsed -15.4% to levels not seen since May 2020. This is the Fed's work. The question is whether Kevin Warsh acknowledges it tomorrow — because if he does, the market gets the dovish pivot narrative it's been waiting for. The data is doing the heavy lifting. Now we find out if Warsh is listening or posturing.
items.tweetB: CPI 4.2%. PPI 6.5%. Housing starts -15.4% to a 5-year low. You can't have it both ways. Either inflation is the problem or growth is cracking. Into Warsh's first FOMC tomorrow, the data just handed him the perfect excuse to not hike — and oil below $80 is the second leg. The dot plot decides everything.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / TMAD / Capital Flows
items.title: Warsh's First FOMC: The Trust Gap, Communication Regime Change, and What Markets Actually Need to Know
items.idea: Kevin Warsh's first FOMC press conference (June 17, 2pm ET) is the real event — the rate decision (97.4% hold) is not. Three separate newsletters frame it the same way: Powell built genuine market trust over years; that trust is now gone. Warsh must earn credibility from zero while facing White House pressure to ease. The market test: Is he independent, or will he bend? If Warsh dismantles the Powell era's padded forward-guidance machine, markets must relearn to price policy without verbal handrails. Capital Flows frames the current moment as a 'credit cycle melt-up' — Warsh's communication style becomes the key variable for duration of that regime.
items.angle: The traders split across the full policy map is the signal — some betting cuts, some betting hikes, some calling for three hikes. That dispersion is unusual and expensive. It means every data point now carries higher vol, and Warsh's clarity (or lack of it) sets the new regime pricing. Long vol going into ambiguous leadership transitions is historically correct.
items.tweetA: Jerome Powell spent 8 years building market trust. Warsh inherits zero of it starting at 2pm today. The question isn't whether rates move — they won't. The question is whether the market believes what Warsh says next. Credibility is not transferred; it's earned. Today is day one.
items.tweetB: If Warsh kills Powell's forward-guidance machine — no more dot-plot handholding, no more 'we'll be patient' verbal insurance — markets have to reprice everything. Shorter vol windows. More data-dependency. Less cushion between policy and prices. That's arguably good for markets long-term. But the transition? That's where you get dislocations.

items.title: Brent Below $80 After 4-Session 15% Slide — The Macro Chain the Market is Underpricing
items.idea: Brent has shed ~15% in four sessions as the Iran deal materialized. The explicit macro transmission chain: lower crude → inflation narrative deflates → bond markets catch bid → Fed tightening path softens → dollar vulnerable → gold reconnects to rate/dollar channel. The 'war premium' in oil was real; now it's draining. But the key observation: the market loves pricing relief but is 'terrible at respecting unresolved risk once the first round of champagne has been poured.' Embers remain — one spark relights the tinderbox.
items.angle: The oil-gold correlation is the cleanest trade here. If oil keeps fading and bonds rally, real yields drop and gold's rates-and-dollar story reasserts itself — more durable than the geopolitical bid. Meanwhile the crack spread normalization is actually a tailwind for refiners ($VLO, $MPC) even as energy producers get hurt.
items.tweetA: Brent -15% in 4 sessions. That's not a blip — that's the entire war-risk premium deflating. The chain: cheaper oil → lower inflation fear → bonds rally → Fed has less pressure → dollar vulnerable → gold reconnects to real yields. I keep staring at the refiner crack spreads. The market is pricing this exactly backwards for $VLO.
items.tweetB: The Middle East peace trade is one of the most reliable landmine setups in markets. Relief is priced immediately. Risk reasserts slowly. We've had ceasefire deals in this region before. The embers don't go cold in 48 hours. Oil below $80 gives the Fed cover. It does not give you a clear runway to ignore geopolitical risk through the summer.

items.title: Dollar Rebounding on Exceptionalism, Not Just War Premium — CFTC Bullish Exposure Biggest Since 2018
items.idea: CFTC positioning data shows bullish dollar exposure surged last week by the most since 2018 to the highest level in more than a year — and this is happening AFTER the Iran peace headlines. The dollar has risen >2% vs a basket of peers since the start of the conflict, but the key tell is that it barely gave back gains when peace headlines hit. The dollar is shifting from war-premium safe haven to US exceptionalism trade: AI boom + resilient data + elevated rates + equity inflows. Important caveat: largest single long-dollar position is USD/JPY carry. May CPI 4.2%, core 2.9%, May jobs 172K. Markets now pricing first Fed move more likely a hike than cut, quarter-point increase by March.
items.angle: This is the dollar story that kills the lazy 'dollar down → gold up' consensus. If capital is rotating into US because of AI-driven earnings and equity inflows, the dollar gets a growth bid — not a fear bid. That's a much harder opponent for gold than a geopolitical hedge. Watch EUR/USD and gold correlation — if they decouple here, the macro regime has shifted.
items.tweetA: CFTC data: bullish dollar exposure just hit the highest level in over a year, rising by the most since 2018. And this happened AFTER the peace deal. The war premium is fading. The dollar isn't. That tells you this isn't fear anymore — it's the AI exceptionalism trade reasserting. Gold bulls, you've been warned.
items.tweetB: 172K jobs added in May. Core inflation 2.9% and rising. Dollar up 2% since the Iran conflict. Markets now pricing a Fed HIKE before a cut. The 'US exceptionalism is over' narrative was priced in February. Turns out the US economy didn't get the memo. At some point that becomes the trade.

items.source: Bloomberg / John Authers + Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Gold Below 200-Day MA for First Time in 3 Years — Structural Bull Still Intact But Easy Narrative Gone
items.idea: Gold traded above its 200-day moving average for three consecutive years — then the Iran deal combined with dollar resilience pushed it below. BofA's global fund manager survey found gold was the most crowded trade as recently as February; it's now marked as 'close to fair value' (not cheap). Classic bear markets bottom well below fair value — so the floor isn't clear. The critical variable is Warsh: if his first dot plot doesn't show tightening and maintains easing bias, gold could rally. But if the dollar trades as a growth asset tied to AI and capital inflows, 'gold will need more than fiscal anxiety and central bank buying to keep the momentum clean.'
items.angle: This is the tension that matters: structural debasement thesis still intact, but the narrative has two sides now. The war-premium tail wind is gone. The clean version of gold as 'anti-dollar' only works if the dollar is being bid for fear reasons. If it's being bid for growth reasons, gold fights uphill. Watch real yields — if they roll over as bonds rally on falling oil, gold gets its oxygen back.
items.tweetA: Gold just broke below its 200-day moving average for the first time in 3 years. Three years. In February it was BofA's most crowded trade. Now it's close to 'fair value' — which in bubble math means it hasn't found the floor yet. The war premium is gone. The easy part of this bull market is over.
items.tweetB: The gold bull case is still structurally intact: fiscal excess, CB buying, de-dollarization. But the market narrative is now two-sided. If the dollar rallies because AI is pulling global capital into US assets — not because of fear — gold has to fight a growth bid, not just a fear bid. That's a different game. I'm still long, but less certain.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Gold price vs 200-day MA showing 3-year breakdown. BofA most-crowded-trade chart. Fund managers positioning on gold as sentiment indicator.

items.title: OpenAI Q1 2026: $5.7B Revenue, $3.7B Cash Burn, $665B Off-Balance-Sheet Compute Commitments
items.idea: The Information published OpenAI's Q1 2026 financials shared with shareholders. Revenue: $5.7B (tripled YoY). Cash burn: $3.7B (>50% of revenue, also tripled YoY). Gross margins: 39% (up from 33% — cost efficiency improving). Cash on hand: $73B (up from $40B at Dec end). Operating loss: $9.3B including $2.3B in SBC. Net loss $21.3B but $12.4B was accounting charge. Company projected $25B burn in 2026 and $57B in 2027. Most striking: $665B in computing spending commitments through 2030 — largely off-balance-sheet. OpenAI has filed confidentially for IPO but 'may take its time.' The kitchen sink strategy is also a marketing move ahead of the IPO given SpaceX's post-IPO success.
items.angle: At $5.7B quarterly revenue = ~$23B annualized run rate and 39% gross margins, this is real scale. But the $665B off-balance-sheet compute commitment is the number that will define the IPO narrative. That's not capex — that's a liability. S-1 will be sobering reading for public investors.
items.tweetA: OpenAI Q1 2026: $5.7B revenue. $3.7B cash burned. Both tripled year-over-year. On the balance sheet: $73B cash. Off the balance sheet: $665 BILLION in compute commitments through 2030. When the S-1 goes public, that footnote will be the whole conversation. The IPO will be the most scrutinized filing in tech history.
items.tweetB: OpenAI is burning more than 50 cents for every dollar of revenue. They're projecting $57B in cash burn in 2027 alone. Gross margins 39% and rising — unit economics improving. But capital intensity accelerating faster. This is the most aggressive infrastructure land-grab since the railroad era. Whether that's genius or catastrophe depends entirely on whether AI monetization catches up to compute cost.

items.title: Microsoft Switches to Cheaper AI Models — Goldman's Trillion-Dollar Question Is Now an Investment Issue
items.idea: Microsoft — 'one of the most important enterprise AI distribution channels in the world' — is looking at lower-cost, open-source AI alternatives to manage enterprise AI costs. This shifts the AI investment debate from 'who builds the smartest model' to 'who delivers useful intelligence at the lowest sustainable cost.' Goldman's trillion-dollar question (can AI capex ever be monetized?) is now a live investment issue. The author's framing: 'the next phase of AI may be less about model scarcity and more about orchestration, efficiency and value capture.' Cheaper intelligence drives more adoption but compresses pricing power investors have been paying for.
items.angle: This is the single most important structural risk to AI model company valuations. If the largest enterprise channel is actively de-risking away from expensive proprietary models, the 'premium model' moat is leaking. Value migrates to orchestration. Winners: open-source infrastructure, inference optimization, custom silicon. Losers: companies whose moat was model-quality-at-premium.
items.tweetA: Microsoft is switching to cheaper open-source AI models to manage enterprise costs. This is not a small developer cutting corners on an API bill. This is THE enterprise distribution channel for AI sending a very clear signal: the era of model scarcity pricing is ending. Goldman's trillion-dollar question just became every tech investor's problem.
items.tweetB: The AI investment thesis in 2023: whoever builds the best model wins. The AI investment thesis in 2026: whoever delivers intelligence cheapest, at scale, with the best integration wins. Microsoft just moved the goalposts. The moat isn't model quality anymore — it's orchestration, efficiency, and lowest cost per useful output. Most AI stocks aren't priced for that world.

items.title: Intel EMIB Breaks TSMC's Advanced Packaging Monopoly — AWS, Google TPU v8e, SpaceX, Tesla Already Signed
items.idea: For three years, TSMC CoWoS was the only game in town for AI accelerator packaging. That monopoly is now cracking. Intel's EMIB has real orders: AWS and Cisco shipping; SpaceX and Tesla signed for Terafab around Q1 2026; Google's TPU v8e reportedly landing on EMIB in H2 2027; Apple, Microsoft, NVIDIA in talks. Intel CFO upgraded external packaging revenue from 'few hundred million' to 'north of a billion' per customer. Total industry CoWoS capacity reaching ~200K wpm by end 2026, with TSMC at ~120K and ASE/Amkor at ~80K. Intel EMIB currently at 8x reticle size vs TSMC CoWoS-L at 5.5x. Rectangular panels vs TSMC's round wafers: at 14x reticle size, Intel fits 6 interposers per panel vs TSMC's 3 — 75% vs 50% area utilization.
items.angle: The packaging bottleneck has been the hidden constraint on AI accelerator scale. If Intel can credibly second-source this at larger sizes, it changes the entire supply chain negotiation dynamic for hyperscalers. Amkor plays both sides regardless. The glass substrate convergence is the 2027-2029 inflection — both TSMC and Intel heading there. LPKF Laser (German company, LIDE process) dominates glass TGV formation.
items.tweetA: TSMC has had a monopoly on AI chip packaging for 3 years. That monopoly is ending. AWS, Cisco, Google's next TPU, SpaceX, Tesla — all signed on Intel's EMIB. Intel CFO: 'billions of dollars per customer.' At 14x reticle size, Intel fits TWICE as many interposers per panel as TSMC due to rectangular vs round format. The era of one-source packaging is over.
items.tweetB: Nobody talks about advanced packaging but it's the actual bottleneck in the AI buildout. Intel EMIB is now a legitimate second source to TSMC CoWoS. Both companies are converging on glass core substrates by 2027-29. The only German company whose LIDE process can drill through-glass vias at scale: LPKF Laser ($LPKF DE). That's a pick-and-shovel in the picks-and-shovels.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Round wafer vs rectangular panel showing 3 vs 6 interposers at 14x reticle. Intel EMIB vs TSMC CoWoS-L size roadmap.

items.title: Glass Substrate Convergence — TSMC and Intel Both Heading to Panels, LPKF LIDE Process Dominates
items.idea: At large package sizes (14x reticle+), organic substrates fail due to warpage and CTE mismatch with silicon. Both TSMC (CoPoS) and Intel are converging on glass core substrates. Key glass properties: high flatness, CTE closer to silicon, low dielectric loss, high Young's modulus, enables through-glass vias (TGVs) closer than organic. Intel demonstrated a 78mm x 77mm glass substrate (7x reticle equivalent). Critical manufacturing challenge 'SeWaRe' (Japanese term for micro-cracking in glass) solved by Intel using LPKF Laser LIDE process. Samsung and Rapidus also entering glass interposer space. A Taiwanese glass partner inside TSMC's CoPoS program is teased (behind paywall).
items.angle: Glass substrates represent the 2027-2030 battleground for advanced packaging. The supply chain for glass processing is extremely concentrated — LPKF LIDE process is effectively the only scaled solution for TGV formation. This is a toll-road business with no bypass. The Taiwanese partner hint suggests TSMC has an exclusive supply relationship Intel doesn't have access to.
items.tweetA: The next advanced packaging battleground isn't silicon — it's glass. At 14x+ reticle sizes, organic substrates warp. Both TSMC and Intel are converging on glass core panels by 2027-2029. One German company's laser process (LPKF LIDE) is the only way to drill through-glass vias at scale. When two $500B+ companies converge on the same material, check the supply chain.
items.tweetB: Glass interposers = the most important semiconductor supply chain nobody is tracking. TSMC's CoPoS and Intel's glass roadmap converge by 2028. Both need through-glass vias (TGVs). There's essentially one company with the production-ready laser process: LPKF Laser ($LPKF DE). Classic monopoly in the enabling layer. Samsung and Rapidus also entering. This matters in 2027.

charts.desc: Diagram of round wafer vs rectangular panel area utilization for large packages.

items.title: SpaceX at $2.75T Forces 'Passive Is No Longer Passive' Crisis — Record 80% of Fund Managers Cite Semis as Most Crowded
items.idea: SpaceX ($2.75T market cap, now 5th-largest company ahead of Amazon at $2.65T) will soon arrive in Nasdaq indexes, creating a forced-buying cascade for passive funds. SocGen's Andrew Lapthorne: top 20 stocks in MSCI AC World are on average 60x bigger than the average of the other 2,500 names — a phenomenon emerging almost entirely post-pandemic. BofA global fund manager survey: record 80% named semiconductors as the world's most crowded trade — most crowded 'most crowded' reading since the survey started. MSCI EM: top 5 (TSMC, Samsung, SK Hynix, Tencent, Alibaba) account for 1/3 of entire 1,205-stock index — more concentrated than S&P 500.
items.angle: The SpaceX entry into indexes is the stress test the concentration problem needed. When a $2.75T company with no track record as a public company lands in your benchmark, you're not making an investment decision — you're making a benchmark compliance decision. The 'curse of the benchmarks' has been building for 15 years. SpaceX just made it impossible to ignore.
items.tweetA: SpaceX joins Nasdaq indexes soon. That means every passive fund must buy it — not because they have a view on rocket valuation, but because benchmark risk is more painful than opinion risk. SocGen's data: top 20 MSCI World stocks are now 60x bigger than the average stock. We're not in a market anymore. We're in a benchmark compliance contest.
items.tweetB: Record 80% of fund managers cite semiconductors as the most crowded trade in history — the most crowded 'most crowded' ever in BofA's survey. Then SpaceX at $2.75T enters the Nasdaq. Passive investors must buy it. Active managers must explain their underweight. Nobody is making investment decisions anymore. Everyone is managing benchmark terror.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Market cap of top 20 stocks as multiple of all others — explosion post-ChatGPT. EAFE vs EM earnings forecast growth gap. BofA crowded trade readings — semis at record 80%. EM vs EAFE outperformance since GFC chart.

items.title: Korea Retail Buys $800M of SpaceX on Day 1 — Beat Micron's 3-Month Total in a Single Session
items.idea: South Korean retail investors bought ~$800M of SpaceX net in its first trading day — making it the most popular US stock among Korea's 14M+ individual investors. For context: Micron attracted ~$748M of Korean retail net buying over THREE MONTHS; a Nasdaq 100 fund pulled ~$700M over the same period. SpaceX beat both in one day. A SpaceX-exposed ETF had already pulled $300M from Korean retail over the prior month — that was the waiting room trade. Korean retail was largely locked out of the IPO itself (Japan/Australia had direct routes); now the Korean regulator is inspecting the access failure. South Korean individuals hold ~$25.9B of Tesla exposure.
items.angle: Korea retail is the world's best real-time sentiment gauge for momentum trades. Their $800M day-1 buy is not 'FOMO retail' — it's technically sophisticated, heavily leveraged, and historically has extended momentum trades for weeks. Blocked IPO demand doesn't disappear, it migrates to secondary with more urgency and less price discipline.
items.tweetA: $800M of Korean retail buying hit SpaceX on day one. Micron got $748M from Korean retail over THREE MONTHS. SpaceX got more in a single session. These aren't passive retail tourists — they're the most aggressive momentum traders in the world. When Korea locks onto a theme this hard, it tends to run further and faster than any valuation model suggests.
items.tweetB: Korean retail was locked out of the SpaceX IPO. Japan and Australia got access. Korea didn't. The result: $800M stormed the secondary market in one session — more than Micron's entire 3-month Korean inflow total. The lesson: block institutional demand at the primary, and it comes back in the secondary with worse price discipline and higher leverage. Every IPO banker should study this.

items.title: BOJ Hikes to 1% First Time in Three Decades — QE Ends April 2027, Yen Still Above 160
items.idea: Bank of Japan raised its target rate to 1% for the first time in three decades. QE will end in April 2027 — the enormous JGB purchases that kept a lid on yields will taper to zero. Minor dovish signal: BOJ replaced 'real interest rates are at significantly low levels' with 'financial conditions are accommodative.' The yen barely moved on the announcement, remaining above 160 vs the dollar. A new Nikkei 225 record reinforces that equity investors think the BOJ isn't done. October rate hike is now a 50/50 shot on futures markets.
items.angle: When QE ends in Japan, the biggest domestic buyer of JGBs disappears. Japanese institutions that have been parked in US Treasuries for yield may begin to repatriate — structural bid for JGBs and structural sell pressure on USTs. The yen-carry trade unwind risk is real (BOJ hike + Warsh hawkish = double-tightening) but the yen staying above 160 tells you the market doesn't believe it yet.
items.tweetA: BOJ hiked to 1% — first time in 30 years. QE ends April 2027. The yen barely moved. That tells you everything: the market doesn't believe the BOJ is serious. When the largest buyer of Japanese government bonds exits the market by next April, someone has to replace them. That demand has to come from somewhere — probably repatriation from US Treasuries. That's the 2027 macro trade no one is pricing.
items.tweetB: The Nikkei hit a new record on the day the BOJ hiked for the first time in three decades. Either Japanese equity investors know something about the yen staying weak, or this is the last easy trade before the carry unwind. October BOJ hike is 50/50 on futures. A Warsh hawkish tone today + another BOJ hike in October is the carry-trade earthquake scenario. Nobody's positioning for it.

charts.desc: BOJ QE taper timeline showing holdings reduction to zero by April 2027. Yen vs dollar chart showing no move on hike day.

items.title: MSCI EM More Concentrated Than S&P 500 — Taiwan + Korea = 49% Weight vs Their 14% of China's GDP
items.idea: MSCI Emerging Markets (1,205 stocks) is now MORE concentrated than the S&P 500. Top 5 stocks (TSMC, Samsung, SK Hynix, Tencent, Alibaba) account for one-third of the entire index. Taiwan + South Korea have combined GDPs of ~$3 trillion — about 14% of China's $21 trillion GDP — yet they hold 49% of the MSCI EM index weight vs China's 21%. DataTrek's Nicholas Colas: 'MSCI EM has been largely captured by two countries.' EM is now essentially a semiconductor/AI infrastructure play. EAFE has only one significant AI play (ASML at 2.85%), while EM chip companies are seeing earnings forecast growth 'at staggering speed.'
items.angle: The MSCI EM vs EAFE divergence is the key trade here. If you're long EM, you're essentially long TSMC and Samsung — meaning you're long AI infrastructure, not 'emerging markets diversification.' EAFE investors are structurally locked out of the AI trade. This creates a clean rotational trade: EM over EAFE for as long as AI capex remains elevated.
items.tweetA: MSCI Emerging Markets (1,205 stocks) is now MORE concentrated than the S&P 500. Taiwan + South Korea are 49% of the EM index despite having 14% of China's GDP. 'Emerging markets diversification' is now just TSMC and Samsung with extra steps. If you're long EM thinking you own Asia exposure — you own the AI semiconductor complex.
items.tweetB: EAFE has ONE significant AI play: ASML at 2.85%. Meanwhile MSCI EM's top 5 chips stocks account for a third of the entire 1,205-stock index. The result: EM outperforming EAFE at the fastest pace since the post-GFC China commodity boom. Except this time it's not macro. It's AI capex. The divergence has years left to run.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: EM vs EAFE outperformance chart showing fastest divergence since GFC. Top 20 MSCI World stocks as multiple of remaining 2,500. EM vs EAFE earnings forecast growth comparison.

items.title: BTC: Four Independent Demand Signals Confirmed — 250K BTC Bought, 11K Off Exchanges, ETF Flows Positive
items.idea: Glassnode shows buyers added 250,000+ BTC between $59,000-$67,000, with Accumulation Trend Score at its strongest of the entire drawdown. Whales pulled 11,000+ BTC off exchanges (classic accumulation signal). Strategy added 1,587 BTC ($100M) to reach 846,842 BTC total. ETF flows turned positive at +$85.8M after 4 straight weeks of outflows totaling $5.4B. BTC opened at $66,287 — its highest level in two weeks — on June 16, leading risk even as Nasdaq fell 1.15%. Key levels: $67,000 = breakout (above 20+50 EMA, below 200 EMA at ~$69K); $64,000 = hawkish-pullback line; $59,100 = structural double-bottom floor.
items.angle: Four independent demand signals pointing the same direction simultaneously is rare. This is not retail momentum — it's institutional accumulation confirming a floor. The key asymmetry: dovish Warsh dot plot = BTC to $67K → $70K; hawkish surprise = $64K retest. The setup has positive expected value. The $59,100 double-bottom is a hard stop.
items.tweetA: Four independent BTC demand signals fired simultaneously: 250K BTC accumulated $59K-$67K (Glassnode), 11K+ BTC pulled off exchanges, Strategy added $100M, ETF flows positive for first time in 4 weeks. BTC opened at a 2-week high even as Nasdaq fell 1.15%. This is the bottom confirmation setup. One gate left: Warsh's dot plot at 2pm today.
items.tweetB: After 4 straight weeks of BTC ETF outflows totaling $5.4B, flows just turned positive at +$85.8M. Strategy bought another $100M. Glassnode shows the strongest accumulation trend score of the entire drawdown. The $59,100 double-bottom held. BTC is leading risk, not following it. The only remaining question is whether Warsh opens the $70K door today.

items.title: XRP: 6 Consecutive Weeks of ETF Inflows = $1.44B — Leading the Institutional Crypto Rotation
items.idea: XRP has logged 6 straight weeks of ETF inflows totaling $1.44B since the November 2025 launch. BlackRock's BITA ETF listed on June 16, adding institutional fuel. This is the exact opposite of BTC and ETH ETF patterns over the same period. ETH exchange supply tightening to ~14.5M; Bitmine holds 5.39M+ ETH. The alt rotation sequence: XRP leading on institution inflows, SOL leading on real on-chain activity (tokenized SpaceX shares on Solana, FIFA World Cup volumes), ETH benefiting from exchange supply squeeze. HYPE +10% on SpaceX IPO trading volume. Stellar (XLM) surged on DTCC tokenized-securities announcement.
items.angle: 6 consecutive weeks of ETF inflows during the same period BTC and ETH bled $5.4B out suggests institutional capital is selectively rotating INTO specific assets, not fleeing crypto. XRP's institutional bid may be related to the CLARITY Act or broader stablecoin/payment narrative. The pattern: big regulated names (BlackRock BITA) choosing XRP as the second major institutional crypto vehicle.
items.tweetA: XRP ETFs have seen 6 straight weeks of inflows = $1.44B, while BTC ETFs bled $5.4B over the same period. BlackRock just listed its BITA ETF on June 16. Institutional capital isn't leaving crypto — it's routing around Bitcoin into XRP specifically. The question is whether that's a regulatory bet, a payments bet, or both.
items.tweetB: The institutional crypto flow rotation: XRP +$1.44B over 6 weeks (inflows), BTC -$5.4B over 4 weeks (outflows). Then both start recovering simultaneously. DTCC announced tokenized securities on Stellar. SpaceX shares being tokenized on Solana. World Cup volumes on-chain. The on-chain activity is now real enough to move prices independently of macro.

items.title: Russia 'Unfriendly Crypto' Bill: 2-3% Commission on USDC + BNB Transactions, Effective July 1
items.idea: Russia's 'unfriendly crypto' bill targets USDC and BNB with 2-3% transaction commissions, effective July 1 — two weeks away. USDC classified as specifically 'unfriendly.' BNB targeted due to Binance's historical exposure to Russian users. VanEck has been specifically noting this as an idiosyncratic headwind for its VBNB ETF. The CLARITY Act (US crypto regulatory framework) is at ~50/50 on prediction markets for passage, with timeline 'not ready by July 4.' USDT presumably tolerated — accelerating the USDC vs USDT geopolitical split.
items.angle: Russia's crypto bill is a signal about global stablecoin fragmentation. USDC as 'unfriendly' while USDT is tolerated accelerates the USDC vs USDT geopolitical split in non-Western markets. For BNB specifically, the Russian commission is a meaningful headwind given Binance's user base. The CLARITY Act delay extends US regulatory uncertainty through summer.
items.tweetA: Russia is slapping a 2-3% commission on USDC and BNB transactions starting July 1. USDC targeted as 'unfriendly.' USDT gets a pass. This is the stablecoin fragmentation trade becoming explicit: the world is splitting into USDC for Western-aligned flows and USDT for everything else. That's not a crypto story — that's a geopolitical infrastructure story.
items.tweetB: July 1: Russia's new crypto bill hits USDC and BNB with 2-3% commissions. CLARITY Act passage uncertain by July 4. EU MiCA is already in effect. The global crypto regulatory map is fragmenting at exactly the moment institutional flows are accelerating. The winner in fragmentation is whoever has neutral jurisdiction — probably why Tether keeps winning.

items.title: DTCC + Stellar (XLM): Tokenized Securities on Public Blockchain — Real-World Asset Adoption Milestone
items.idea: DTCC — the Depository Trust and Clearing Corporation, the central clearinghouse for US securities processing trillions daily — announced a tokenized securities initiative on the Stellar (XLM) network. Stellar surged immediately. BlackRock also listed its BITA ETF on June 16, adding further institutional-grade infrastructure. SpaceX shares are being tokenized and traded on Solana — real volumes, not test transactions. HYPE +10% on SpaceX IPO trading volume on-chain.
items.angle: DTCC choosing Stellar is the most important RWA announcement in the cycle. DTCC processes $2.5 quadrillion in securities annually. If even 0.1% moves on-chain through Stellar, XLM becomes infrastructure, not speculation. Stellar is public permissioned — that's why DTCC can do this without building their own chain.
items.tweetA: DTCC — the plumbing of US securities markets, processing trillions daily — just announced tokenized securities on Stellar. Not a pilot. Not a sandbox. The actual clearinghouse. This is to RWA what the first internet bank was to payments: the moment the incumbent chose the new rails. XLM just became infrastructure.
items.tweetB: Three things happening simultaneously in settlement infrastructure: DTCC on Stellar, SpaceX shares tokenized on Solana, BlackRock listing BITA on-chain. The 'blockchain for finance' thesis always needed real volume from real institutions. That volume is now arriving. Stellar's advantage: public permissioned network that doesn't require DTCC to trust a private chain's validators.

items.title: Private Credit at a Crossroads: Easy Gains Fading, AI's Impact on Software Borrowers Creates New Scrutiny
items.idea: McKinsey's Global Private Markets Report 2026 flags a structural shift: private credit returns have moderated as interest rate cuts reduced lending income and loan defaults normalized. Some funds have marked down loan values, trimmed dividends, or faced increased redemption pressure. The specific new risk: AI's potential impact on software companies — which have been private credit's biggest borrowers — is leading to increased scrutiny of underwriting and due diligence. Deal volumes were near historic highs in 2025; strategies beyond direct lending are expanding; fundraising remains resilient. 'Private credit is becoming less about deploying capital at pace and more about deploying it with precision.'
items.angle: The AI threat to software company borrowers is the most underappreciated risk in private credit. If AI coding agents deliver 3-5x productivity, SaaS headcount shrinks, revenue growth slows, and the DCF models that justified private credit loans to software companies start looking stale. This is a slow-motion credit event, not an immediate one.
items.tweetA: McKinsey's private credit report has a buried warning: AI's impact on software companies — private credit's biggest borrowers — is creating 'increased scrutiny of underwriting.' Think about that loop: AI makes software companies more efficient → headcount drops → revenue growth slows → the DCF that justified the private credit loan starts looking different. The easy gains in private credit are over.
items.tweetB: Private credit had a golden era of easy gains. That era is ending. Rate cuts reduced lending income. Defaults normalized. And now the biggest borrower category — software companies — faces AI disruption to their business model. Private credit went from 'spread the capital fast' to 'underwrite with precision.' That's a regime change.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (McKinsey Global Private Markets Report 2026)

items.title: Eight Drivers of Extraordinary US Profit Growth — 40% Came From Falling Taxes + Interest Alone
items.idea: NY Fed economist Michael Smolyansky (2023): the reduction in corporate interest and tax expenses alone explained over 40% of real corporate profit growth from 1989 to 2019. Federal corporate tax revenues are now at their LOWEST POINT in the post-WW2 period, with deficits running >6% of GDP and debt >100% GDP. Eight discrete factors have 'all rowed the earnings boat in unison': (1) Falling corporate tax rates, (2) Falling cost of capital, (3) Tech-heavy S&P composition, (4) Dovish Fed, (5) Excess fiscal spending, (6) Labor productivity, (7) Globalization, (8) Industry consolidation/monopolies. The author is 'humbly dubious' that AI will overwhelm the historical cyclicality of productivity. Street is pricing double-digit earnings growth for next 2 years into stocks now.
items.angle: The most important insight: if 40% of the profit supercycle since 1989 was simply taxes + interest going down — and both are now inflecting the wrong way — the remaining 60% of the story needs to work extremely hard. This is the bear case for S&P margins that doesn't require a recession, just mean reversion on two secular tailwinds.
items.tweetA: One NY Fed paper changed my view on US corporate profits: 40% of real profit growth from 1989-2019 came entirely from falling interest rates and corporate taxes. Both tailwinds are now gone or reversing. The market is pricing double-digit EPS growth for two more years. What's doing the work that taxes and rates can no longer do? AI productivity. I hope that thesis is right, because the math needs it.
items.tweetB: US corporate tax revenues are at their lowest level since WWII. Interest expenses are rising. Deficits running >6% of GDP. And the market expects double-digit earnings growth for the next 2 years. The 1989-2019 profit supercycle had 8 tailwinds rowing in unison. Several have stalled or reversed. 'Earnings durability' is the question nobody wants to answer carefully.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Eric Basmajian chart on corporate profit drivers — decomposition of sources over the profit supercycle.

items.title: AI Coding Agents: 3-5x Productivity in Early Adopters — Sonar Achieves 50-80% Gains
items.idea: McKinsey finds early adopters of AI coding agents seeing 3-5x productivity improvements when they don't just adopt tools but 'rewire their software delivery models, operating practices, and governance.' In one documented pilot, software company Sonar redesigned its product development life cycle using an AI-native framework and achieved 50-80% productivity gains. The role of software engineers is evolving from writing code to 'supervising, validating, and orchestrating AI-generated outputs.' Key insight: 'near-continuous software delivery' is the new outcome these companies are targeting.
items.angle: 50-80% productivity gains in software development = massive implications for SaaS headcount, R&D cost structures, and software company margins. If this scales broadly, software companies could deliver the same output with 40-50% fewer engineers in 2-3 years. That's deflationary for software company costs and potentially inflationary for software company margins — but also affects their private credit lenders.
items.tweetA: McKinsey: early AI coding agent adopters seeing 3-5x productivity improvements. Sonar redesigned its entire dev lifecycle and hit 50-80% gains. These aren't chatbot-assisted productivity bumps. These are complete rewires of how software is built. The companies not doing this are building technical debt at 1x speed while competitors move at 4x. This is the SaaS competitive moat question of the decade.
items.tweetB: Software engineers are becoming AI supervisors. McKinsey documents 3-5x productivity for early AI coding adopters. The implication nobody wants to say out loud: you can build the same software product with half the engineers. That's deflationary for software company costs and bullish for software margins — unless everyone does it at once, which collapses software pricing. Interesting game theory.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom (referencing Peter Oppenheimer / Goldman Sachs)
items.title: Goldman's Capex Boom Framework: The Market is Rewiring to Avoid the Next Kodak, Nokia, and BlackBerry
items.idea: Peter Oppenheimer at Goldman Sachs has produced what Dark Side of the Boom calls 'one of the best big-picture market frameworks I have read in years.' The central thesis: the capex boom is fundamentally rewiring market structure, and investors are making a categorical shift — specifically trying to avoid owning 'the next Kodak, Polaroid, Nokia or BlackBerry.' This is not just about picking winners in AI; it's about avoiding the incumbents who will be disrupted BY AI capex winners. The framework elevates the disruption risk story above the valuation story for legacy tech.
items.angle: The 'avoid the next Nokia' framing is more powerful than people realize. It creates asymmetric selling pressure on incumbents perceived as disruption risks, independent of their current earnings. Companies with high terminal value uncertainty get sold even if near-term earnings are strong. This is the mechanism explaining why profitable legacy tech names are re-rating lower in bull markets.
items.tweetA: Goldman's Peter Oppenheimer calls the capex boom 'rewiring the market.' The specific fear driving positioning: nobody wants to own the next Kodak, Polaroid, Nokia or BlackBerry. This isn't a valuation trade. It's a survivorship trade. Investors are selling perceived disruption risk regardless of current earnings. That's a different market regime than anything since the dot-com era.
items.tweetB: The Goldman capex boom framework: the market's primary concern isn't valuation — it's disruption survival. Will you benefit from AI capex, or be disrupted by the company that benefits? Incumbents with unclear AI positioning are being sold not because they're expensive, but because their terminal value is uncertain. That's a regime change in how markets price risk.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Goldman Sachs, paid access)

items.title: AI Flipping B2B Sales Power: Procurement Uses AI to Benchmark Prices — Vendor Advantages Eroding
items.idea: McKinsey's 2026 Global B2B Pulse Survey: AI is changing the balance of power in B2B sales as procurement teams increasingly use AI tools to compare vendors, analyze contracts, and benchmark pricing. Traditional vendor advantages are eroding as buyers gather and compare information more easily. Leading suppliers respond by making value easier to verify through transparency and structured data. McKinsey finding: leading B2B performers are 4x more likely than peers to deliver one-to-one personalization and 2x more likely to have fully adopted AI. Omnichannel + e-commerce presence are now 'baseline expectations, not differentiators.'
items.angle: If AI procurement tools commoditize B2B vendor comparison, pricing power in enterprise software and services deflates fast. The only defense is hyperpersonalization that's harder to commoditize than pricing. This has direct implications for software companies' enterprise renewal rates and the premium they can charge.
items.tweetA: Procurement teams now use AI to compare vendors, analyze contracts, and benchmark pricing in real time. McKinsey: traditional vendor advantages are eroding. The B2B sales playbook that worked for 40 years — relationships + information asymmetry — is breaking. The new game: prove your value quantitatively or lose the bid. Enterprise pricing power just got structurally weaker.
items.tweetB: McKinsey's B2B survey: leading performers are 4x more likely to personalize at scale and 2x more likely to have adopted AI. The laggards think omnichannel is still a differentiator. It's table stakes now. When AI makes every customer as informed as the best sales analyst, the only sustainable moat is knowing the customer better than any AI can aggregate. That's a data moat, not a relationship moat.

items.title: SpaceX Announced AI Acquisition Hours After June 16 Close — Vertical AI Ambitions Confirmed
items.idea: SpaceX announced an AI acquisition in the hours after market close on June 16 — the same day it passed Amazon in market cap to become the world's 5th-largest company. This confirms SpaceX is building vertical AI capabilities. SpaceX already has Starlink (the world's largest satellite internet network) as a data collection and AI training moat. An AI acquisition immediately after its public debut signals the company is building integrated AI-to-space infrastructure. This positions SpaceX as a potential competitor to the hyperscalers in specialized AI inference for defense, satellite, and autonomous systems.
items.angle: SpaceX acquiring AI capabilities while trading at $2.75T creates a unique narrative: it's not an AI infrastructure stock being valued on capex, it's an operational company (Starlink revenues, launch revenues) that's adding AI capabilities on top of a defensible moat. If SpaceX builds AI that optimizes satellite networks and launch operations, the moat compounds. This is the story that justifies the multiple to passive investors who must buy it.
items.tweetA: SpaceX went public, passed Amazon in market cap, then announced an AI acquisition — all in 3 days. If Starlink is the world's largest private satellite network AND the training data moat for space + defense AI, the $2.75T valuation starts to have an internal logic. This isn't just rockets anymore. It's AI infrastructure with a launch vehicle attached.
items.tweetB: The AI acquisition announcement came hours after SpaceX surpassed Amazon to become the 5th-largest company in the world. Sequence matters: go public → hit $2.75T → immediately signal you're building AI capabilities. SpaceX with vertical AI = potential hyperscaler competitor for defense, satellite, and autonomous systems. Index funds have to own it at any price.

items.title: Nasdaq-100 June 22 Rebalance: CoreWeave, Nebius, Astera Labs, Rocket Lab, Teradyne Added
items.idea: The Nasdaq-100 quarterly rebalance on June 22 adds: Astera Labs, CoreWeave, Nebius, Rocket Lab, and Teradyne. Marvell Technology is also joining the S&P 500 later this month. CoreWeave (GPU cloud infrastructure) and Nebius (European AI cloud) are the most significant additions from an AI infrastructure standpoint. Astera Labs (PCIe connectivity for AI systems) is a key picks-and-shovels play. These additions trigger automatic buying from all Nasdaq-100-indexed funds — trillions in AUM must rebalance into these names within a very short window.
items.angle: Rebalance additions are one of the cleanest short-term flows trades in markets. The buying happens over a defined 3-5 day window and the price impact is measurable. CoreWeave and Nebius as AI infrastructure plays getting mandatory passive buying is a tailwind on top of the AI capex narrative.
items.tweetA: Nasdaq-100 adds CoreWeave, Nebius, Astera Labs, Rocket Lab, and Teradyne on June 22. Trillions in indexed AUM must buy these names in a 3-5 day window. CoreWeave and Nebius are the AI infrastructure plays. Astera Labs is the PCIe connectivity pick-and-shovel. This is the forced-buying event that follows the SpaceX forced-buying event. The passive complex is becoming an AI capex allocation machine.
items.tweetB: Marvell joining S&P 500. CoreWeave and Nebius joining Nasdaq-100 this month. These aren't sector rotations — they're structural additions to the indices that own trillions. Every dollar that tracks these benchmarks now automatically owns AI cloud infrastructure. The feedback loop: AI capex → profitable AI companies → index inclusion → mandatory passive buying → capital available for more AI capex.
